strInstructions: Finely chop the peppers in a food processor. Then, tip them into a sieve and press them to release all of their juices. Transfer the juices into a bowl along with the mince, red pepper paste, pul biber, 1½ tsp flaky sea salt, and 2 tbsp of the oil. Mix all ingredients together, kneading well for at least 2–3 minutes. If necessary, wet your hands with cold water to prevent the mixture from sticking. The mixture should be sticky when finished. Cover and chill for at least 2 hours, or up to 12 hours.

When ready to cook, heat the grill to high or the oven to 220°C/200°C fan/gas 6. Divide the mixture into 12 equal portions, around 85g each. If you would like to skewer them, divide the mixture into 8 equal portions and roll them into balls. Using wet hands, thread the balls onto the end of the skewers, massaging the mixture down the skewers in between your palms until it is evenly distributed. Ensure that the mixture is fully wrapped tightly around the skewers, without any exposed metal. Alternatively, lay them on a large baking tray lined with parchment paper if cooking in the oven, or foil if cooking under the grill. Shape them into 20 cm-long köfte. Wet your fingers with a little cold water and make indents all along the köfte for the traditional shape.

Gently brush each köfte with the remaining 1 tbsp of oil. Cook under the grill, on the top shelf for 10–12 minutes, turning regularly, or cook in the oven for 16–18 minutes, until crispy on the outside and juicy in the middle.

Alternative Pan Fry Method: To pan fry the egg rolls, add enough oil to a medium skillet with high walls so the oil reaches about 1/2 inch up the side of the skillet. Heat the oil on medium-high heat until it reaches 350°F. Add the egg rolls and fry for 3 to 4 minutes. Flip the rolls and fry for another 3 to 4 minutes until golden brown. Remove the egg rolls and let them drain and cool on a few paper towels.

Cook the filling: In a large skillet over medium heat, add the olive oil along with the ground pork or chicken. Break apart the meat with a spatula or wooden spoon as it cooks, and cook until the meat is cooked through (6 to 8 minutes).

Next, add the garlic, ginger, carrot, scallions, and cabbage. Continue to cook until the cabbage wilts down and is soft (another 3 to 4 minutes), stirring regularly. Season the filling with soy sauce and rice wine vinegar, and take off the heat to cool. (This filling can be made in advance.)

Assemble the egg rolls: Place a single egg roll wrapper on a dry surface, positioning it so one point of the square is facing you (like a diamond). Place about 1/4 cup of the egg roll filling mixture in the center of the wrapper.

Dip your fingers in water and run your fingers around the edges of the wrapper. Fold the edges of the wrapper over the center and begin rolling the egg roll away from you to form a tight cylinder. Place the finished roll on a plate and repeat until you have used all the filling. (You should get at least a dozen egg rolls.)

Air fry the egg rolls: Place the egg rolls in the air fryer basket. Spray or brush them lightly with oil. Add as many egg rolls as possible without stacking them or allowing them to touch, ensuring the air can circulate around them. Place the basket in the air fryer and set the air fryer to 350°F.

Cook for 6 to 7 minutes. Then, flip the egg rolls, spray or brush them with oil a second time on the bottom side, and cook for another 4 to 5 minutes. The finished egg rolls should be golden brown and crispy. Serve immediately.

Soak the potatoes in just-boiled water for 30 minutes. Drain, and then leave them to air-dry for 5 minutes. Heat the air fryer to 200C. Tip the potatoes into a bowl, drizzle over 1 tbsp of the oil, and add 1/2 tsp each of salt and freshly ground black pepper. Mix to coat the potatoes all over, then tip into the air fryer basket and cook for 20–30 minutes until crisp and golden.

Meanwhile, heat the remaining oil in a small pan over medium-low heat and fry the onion for 8–10 minutes until softened but not golden. Stir in the garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the paprika and cook for 30 seconds more. Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 1 minute, then tip in the chopped tomatoes. Cook for 5–10 minutes over medium heat until slightly thickened.
Once the potatoes are cooked, tip them out onto a platter and pour over the tomato sauce. Sprinkle with the basil leaves, then serve.
Tip the bread into a bowl and pour over 350ml of water. Let it soak for 10 minutes.
Blend the ingredients together with 350ml of water and 1 tsp salt.
Let the soup cool in the fridge for about 1 hour, then serve with a drizzle of oil and some black pepper.
**Make the Dough:** Cream the butter and sugar. Add the egg yolks and lemon zest. Gradually mix in the flour and cornstarch until a dough forms. Chill the dough for 1 hour.
**Bake the Cookies:** Roll out the dough and cut it into circles. Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 12–15 minutes. Let the cookies cool completely.
**Assemble:** Spread dulce de leche on one cookie, then sandwich it with a second cookie. Roll the edges in coconut flakes.
**Pro Tips:** Chill the dough before rolling it out to make it easier to handle and to prevent the cookies from spreading too much while baking.
**Pro Tips:** Dip the alfajores in melted chocolate and let them set on a wire rack for an extra decadent treat.

meals.strMeal: Algerian Bouzgene Berber Bread with Roasted Pepper Sauce
strInstructions: Preheat the oven's broiler. Place the red bell peppers and tomatoes on a baking sheet and roast under the broiler for about 8 minutes, turning occasionally. This should blacken the skin and help it peel off more easily. Cool, then scrape the skins off the tomatoes and peppers, and place them in a large bowl. Remove cores and seeds from the bell peppers.

Heat 1 tablespoon of olive oil in a skillet over medium heat. Add the jalapeños and garlic and cook until tender, stirring frequently. Remove from heat, and transfer the garlic and jalapeños to the bowl with the tomatoes and red peppers. Using two sharp steak knives (one in each hand), cut up the tomatoes and peppers to a coarse and soupy consistency. Stir, and set the sauce aside.

Place the semolina in a large bowl, and stir in salt and 4 tablespoons of olive oil. Gradually add water while mixing and squeezing with your hand until the dough holds together without being sticky or dry, and molds easily with the hand. Divide into 6 pieces and form into balls.

For each round, heat 1 tablespoon of olive oil in a large heavy skillet over medium heat. Roll out dough one round at a time, to no thicker than 1/4 inch. Fry in the hot skillet until dark brown spots appear on the surface and they are crispy. Remove from the skillet, and wrap in a clean towel while preparing the remaining flatbreads.
To eat the bread and sauce, break off pieces of the bread and scoop them into the sauce. It will slide off, but just keep reaching in!
Place a steamer insert into a saucepan and fill it with 1 1/2 cups of water, or just below the bottom of the steamer. Cover and bring the water to a boil over high heat.
Add the sliced carrots, reduce the heat to medium, and cover the pan again. Steam until tender but not mushy (4 to 6 minutes, depending on the thickness of the slices).
Reserve 1/2 cup of the cooking liquid.

Heat the olive oil in a skillet over medium heat. Reduce the heat to low and stir in the salt, pepper, cinnamon, cumin, garlic, and thyme. Cook the spices and garlic, stirring frequently, until fragrant (about 10 minutes).
Add the 1/2 cup reserved cooking liquid and the bay leaf, cover, and simmer for 20 minutes.
Stir in the carrots, tossing well to coat with the spice mixture, and cook until heated through (about 2 to 3 minutes).
Sprinkle with lemon juice and remove the bay leaf before serving.

Preheat the oven to 450 degrees F (230 degrees C). Place the whole peppers on aluminum foil and bake until the skin is spotted black and the peppers are soft (30 to 45 minutes), turning the peppers once if necessary.
Remove the peppers from the oven and set aside to cool for 10 minutes. Peel off the skin, remove the stem and seeds, and chop the roasted peppers into half-inch pieces.
Heat the olive oil in a skillet over medium heat. Stir in the onion and cook, stirring frequently, until the onion has softened and turned translucent (about 5 minutes).

Add the garlic, salt, and pepper. Stir in the chopped peppers and tomato. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the tomato is soft and the mixture is well incorporated (about 5 minutes).

Combine ground beef, 1/2 of the minced garlic, and 1 tablespoon chopped onion in a large bowl. Mix with your hands until fully incorporated. Shape the meat mixture into 1 1/2-inch oblong patties; you should have 12 to 14 meatballs.

Heat a large skillet over medium-high heat. Brown the patties in batches in the hot skillet until crispy on both sides and no longer pink in the center (about 10 minutes). Set the meatballs aside in a rimmed serving dish.

Reduce heat to medium and stir the remaining chopped onion into the drippings in the skillet. Season with salt and pepper. Cook, stirring constantly, until the onion has softened and turned translucent (about 5 minutes).
Stir in the remaining garlic and cook for 30 seconds. Stir in the Roma tomatoes, dried parsley, and ras el hanout. Pour in water. Cook until the tomatoes are soft (about 5 minutes).
Pour tomato sauce over the meatballs to serve.

Preheat the oven to 180°C/fan 160°C/gas 4. In a bowl, combine the oats, coconut, flour, and sugar. In a small pan, melt the butter and stir in the golden syrup. Separately, add the bicarbonate of soda to 2 tbsp of boiling water, then stir this liquid into the golden syrup and butter mixture.
Make a well in the center of the dry ingredients and pour in the butter and golden syrup mixture. Stir gently until the dry ingredients are fully incorporated.

Place dessertspoonfuls of the mixture onto buttered baking sheets, spacing them about 2.5 cm/1 inch apart to allow room for spreading. Bake in batches for 8–10 minutes until golden brown. Transfer to a wire rack to cool.
Preheat an outdoor grill for medium-high heat and lightly oil the grate. Prick the surface of the skin of the eggplants several times with the tip of a knife.

Place the eggplants directly on the grill. Turn frequently with tongs while the skin chars. Cook until the eggplants have collapsed and are very soft, 25 to 30 minutes. Transfer to a bowl, cover tightly with aluminum foil, and allow to cool for about 15 minutes.
When the eggplants are cool enough to handle, split them in half and scrape the flesh into a colander placed over a bowl. Drain for 5 or 10 minutes.

Transfer the eggplant flesh to a mixing bowl. Add the crushed garlic and salt, and mash until creamy but with a little texture, about 5 minutes. Whisk in the lemon juice, tahini, olive oil, and cayenne pepper. Stir in the yogurt.
Cover the bowl with plastic wrap and refrigerate until completely chilled. Stir in the mint and parsley, and taste to adjust seasonings before serving.

Rinse the baingan (eggplant or aubergine) in water. Pat dry with a kitchen napkin. Apply oil all over and keep it for roasting on an open flame. You can also grill the baingan or roast it in the oven, but then you won't get the smoky flavor of the baingan. Keep the eggplant turning for 2 to 3 minutes on the flame so that it is evenly cooked. You could also embed some garlic cloves in the baingan and then roast it.

Roast the aubergine until it is completely cooked and tender. Check the doneness with a knife; the knife should slide easily into the aubergine without any resistance. Remove the baingan and immerse it in a bowl of water until it cools down.

You can also perform the dhungar technique to infuse smoky charcoal flavor into the baingan. This is an optional step. Use natural charcoal for this method. Heat a small piece of charcoal over a flame until it becomes smoking hot and red.
Make small cuts on the baingan with a knife. Place the red-hot charcoal on the same plate where the roasted aubergine is kept. Add a few drops of oil to the charcoal; it will begin to smoke.

As soon as smoke begins to release from the charcoal, cover the entire plate tightly with a large bowl. Allow the charcoal smoke to infuse for 1 to 2 minutes. The more you do, the more smoky the baingan bharta will become. I suggest keeping it for a minute. Alternatively, you can perform this dhungar method once the baingan bharta is cooked, just like the way we do for Dal Tadka.
Peel the skin from the roasted and smoked eggplant.
Chop the cooked eggplant finely or you can even mash it.
In a kadai or pan, heat oil. Then add finely chopped onions and garlic.
Sauté the onions until translucent. Don't brown them.
Add chopped green chilies and sauté for a minute.
Add the chopped tomatoes and mix it well.
Bhuno (saute) the tomatoes until the oil starts separating from the mixture.
Now add the red chili powder. Stir and mix well.
Add the chopped cooked baingan.
Stir and mix the chopped baingan very well with the onion-tomato masala mixture.
Season with salt. Stir and sauté for another 4 to 5 minutes.

Finally, stir in the coriander leaves with the baingan bharta or garnish it with them. Serve Baingan Bharta with phulkas, rotis, or chapatis. It goes well even with bread, toasted or grilled bread, and plain rice or jeera rice.
Preheat the oven to 180C/fan 160C/gas 4.
Trim the fronds from the fennel and set them aside.
Cut the fennel bulbs in half, then cut each half into 3 wedges.
Cook the fennel wedges in boiling salted water for 10 minutes, then drain well.
Chop the fennel fronds roughly, then mix them with the parsley and lemon zest.
Spread the drained fennel over a shallow ovenproof dish, then add the tomatoes.
Drizzle with olive oil, and bake for 10 minutes.
Nestle the salmon among the vegetables, sprinkle with lemon juice, and bake for 15 minutes more, or until the fish is just cooked.
Scatter the mixture over the parsley and serve.
To make the pastry, measure the flour into a bowl and rub in the butter with your fingertips until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the water and mix to form a soft dough.
Roll out the dough on a lightly floured work surface and use it to line a 20cm/8in flan tin. Chill in the fridge for 30 minutes.
Preheat the oven to 200C/400F/Gas 6 (180C fan).
Line the pastry case with foil and fill with baking beans. Bake blind for about 15 minutes. Then, remove the beans and foil and cook for a further five minutes to dry out the base.
For the filing, spread the base of the flan generously with raspberry jam.
Melt the butter in a pan. Take off the heat and then stir in the sugar. Add the ground almonds, egg, and almond extract. Pour the mixture into the flan tin and sprinkle with flaked almonds.
Bake for about 35 minutes. If the almonds seem to be browning too quickly, cover the tart loosely with foil to prevent them from burning.

meals.strMeal: Baklava with spiced nuts, ricotta & chocolate
strInstructions: First, make the syrup. Tip the sugar into a large saucepan with 650ml water. Stir over a low heat until the sugar has dissolved, then turn up the heat and bring to a boil. Reduce the heat to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes. Squeeze in a few drops of lemon juice and simmer for a further 5 minutes. Remove from the heat and leave to cool. Meanwhile, for assembling the baklava later, melt the butter in a small pan over a low heat for 5 minutes, skimming and discarding any froth that rises to the surface.

For the filling, crush all of the nuts in a pestle and mortar, or blitz them in a food processor—you want a mixture of finely ground nuts with a few chunky pieces. Tip into a bowl, stir through the spices, and set aside.

In a separate bowl, mix the ricotta with the lemon and orange zests and vanilla. Heat the oven to 180°C/160°C fan/gas 4. Brush the bottom of a large baking tray (about 35 x 47cm) with some of the melted butter. Working with one sheet of filo at a time (covering the rest with a damp tea towel to prevent it drying out), lay the sheet out on a board so one of the short ends is facing you. Sprinkle 30g of the nut mixture evenly over the whole sheet, then spoon 1 tbsp of the ricotta mixture across the end closest to you. Fold this end over to enclose the filling, then lay a long, thin skewer next to the folded edge and roll the pastry around it to create a long roll. When it’s fully rolled up, it should be roughly the thickness of a chipolata sausage. While holding one end of the rolling pin or skewer, gently scrunch the filo roll like an accordion and carefully push it off the skewer and onto the prepared tray. Repeat with the rest of the filo and fillings—you should get about 12 rolls. Cut each roll into four to make 48 large baklavas, or eight to make 96 mini.

Brush with the remaining melted butter. Bake for 20-25 minutes until evenly golden, turning the tray around halfway through. While still hot, immediately pour over 5-6 ladlefuls of the syrup. You should hear the syrup sizzle as it hits the hot baklava. Set aside to cool and absorb.

Melt the dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of simmering water, ensuring the bowl doesn’t touch the water, or in the microwave in short bursts. Drizzle this over the cooled baklava and sprinkle with the ground pistachios.
Mash the banana with a fork in a bowl until it resembles a thick purée.
Stir in the eggs, baking powder, and vanilla.
Heat a large non-stick frying pan or pancake pan over medium heat and brush with half the oil.
Using half of the batter, spoon two pancakes into the pan. Cook for 1–2 minutes on each side, then tip them onto a plate.
Repeat the process with the remaining oil and batter.
Top the pancakes with the pecans and raspberries.
Cook the noodles following the package instructions. Rinse under cold water and drain thoroughly.
In a small saucepan, melt together the peanut butter, coconut milk, sweet chilli sauce, and half the spring onions. Add 1–2 tbsp of water to loosen the mixture to a drizzling consistency.
Mix the noodles, cucumber, and beansprouts in a serving dish. Top with the prawns, drizzle over the peanut sauce, and scatter over the remaining spring onions.

Preheat the oven to 200°C/fan 180°C/gas. In a large bowl, mix the sugar into the bread mix, then add water according to the package instructions. Bring the dough together with a wooden spoon, and then knead on a lightly floured surface for 5 minutes until smooth. Place the dough into a large bowl, cover with oiled cling film, and let it rest in a warm place until doubled in size.

Meanwhile, heat the oil in a pan and fry the bacon until crisp, about 5 minutes. Add the ginger and garlic and fry for 1 minute until soft. Next, pour in the soy, honey, and tomato purée and stir well. This mixture can be made up to 3 days ahead.

Turn out the dough and knead briefly. Then, pull the dough into 12 even-sized balls. Flatten each ball with your hands and place a teaspoon-sized blob of the filling in the center. Carefully draw the dough up and pinch it closed like a purse. Turn the finished bun over and place it on a large baking sheet. Cover with oiled cling film and allow it to rise for about 30 minutes, or until the dough feels pillowy. Brush the tops with beaten egg and bake for 20 minutes, or until golden brown. Serve warm with dipping sauce. These can be frozen after the second rise for up to 1 month, or baked up to a day ahead and reheated.

Tip all the dressing ingredients into a food processor with a pinch of salt and blitz until a dressing forms. Slash the fish three times on each side, coat with half of the dressing, and set aside to marinate for about 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C (fan 200°C / gas 7). Place the fish on a roasting tray and cook in the oven for 20 minutes, or until the flesh is firm and the eyes have turned white. Serve the fish with the rest of the dressing and steamed couscous or rice.

Cooking barramundi on the bone, as done here, has several advantages: it will stay more moist during cooking, and some suggest the flavor is also enhanced. If you wish to remove the bones, they are easy to locate and less likely to be lodged in the fillet if the fish is cooked whole. Fillets can be simply pan-fried or grilled. If you like trout, you will enjoy the flavor of barramundi, which lends itself to similar ingredients and cooking methods; citrus flavors, garlic, and wild mushrooms are particularly good pairings. Simply roasting the fish with fresh herbs, olive oil, and seasoning is delicious, and in the summer months, you can also barbecue it. One thing you mustn't miss are the cheeks, or 'pearls,' of the fish—they are simply lovely, moist, and sweet, making them well worth leaving the head on for.
Preheat the oven to 180ÀC / 160ÀC fan / gas 4. Line the base and sides of a 20cm square tin with baking parchment (the easiest way is to cross two 20cm-long strips over the base).

To make the almond sponge, place the butter, sugar, flour, ground almonds, baking powder, eggs, vanilla, and almond extract in a large bowl. Beat with an electric whisk until the mixture comes together smoothly.

Scrape the mixture into the tin, spreading it to the corners, and bake for 25–30 minutes, or until a skewer poked into the sponge comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack to finish cooling while you prepare the second sponge.

For the pink sponge, line the tin as above. Mix all the ingredients together as previously instructed, but do not add the almond extract. Fold in some pink food colouring. Then, scrape the mixture into the tin and bake as before. Cool.

To assemble, heat the jam in a small pan until runny, then sieve it. Gently trim two opposite edges from the almond sponge, and then trim a third edge. Roughly measure the height of the sponge, then, using a ruler, cut 4 slices from the well-trimmed edge, ensuring each slice is the same width as the sponge height. Discard or nibble any leftover sponge. Repeat this process with the pink cake.

Take 2 almond slices and 2 pink slices, and trim them so they are all the same length. Roll out one marzipan block on a surface lightly dusted with icing sugar to just over 20cm wide, then continue rolling lengthways until the marzipan is roughly 0.5cm thick.

Brush the rolled marzipan with apricot jam. At one end, lay a pink slice and an almond slice side by side, brushing jam in between to stick the sponges, and leaving 4cm of clear marzipan at the end. Brush more jam over the top of the sponges, then sandwich the remaining 2 slices on top, alternating colors to create a checkerboard effect. Trim the marzipan to the length of the cakes.

Carefully lift the marzipan and smooth the cake with your hands, leaving a small marzipan fold along the bottom edge before sticking it to the first side. Trim the opposite side to match the size of the fold, then crimp the edges using your fingers and thumb (or, more simply, press with the prongs of a fork). If desired, mark the 10 slices using the prongs of a fork.
Assemble the second Battenberg and keep it in an airtight box or well-wrapped in cling film for up to 3 days. It can be frozen for up to a month.
Add the butter to a large Dutch oven or other heavy-duty pot over medium heat. When the butter has melted, add the onion and sauté until translucent.
Add the cabbage, carrot, and celery. Sauté until the vegetables begin to soften, stirring frequently, for about 3 minutes.
Add the bay leaf and vegetable stock and bring to a boil over high heat. Reduce the heat to low and simmer, covered, until the vegetables are crisp-tender, about 15 minutes.
Add the potatoes and bring the mixture back to a boil over high heat. Reduce the heat to low and simmer, covered, until the potatoes are tender, about 10 minutes.
Add the tomatoes (or undrained canned tomatoes) and bring the soup back to a boil over high heat. Reduce the heat to low and simmer, uncovered, for 5 minutes. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
Remove and discard the bay leaf from the pot.
Serve topped with fresh sour cream and fresh dill.

Place a sieve over a large bowl. Line the sieve with a thick sheet of non-dyed kitchen paper or a clean muslin cloth, and spoon in the yogurt. Cover with another sheet of kitchen paper and let it strain in the fridge for a minimum of 12 hours.

Add the lemon juice, most of the olive oil, and the dried mint to a bowl and stir well until the dried mint softens and soaks up the juices. Mix in the strained yogurt. Then, pour away the liquid from the strained yogurt and set that bowl aside.

Halve the cucumber(s) lengthways and remove the seeds by running a teaspoon from the top to the bottom of the flesh. Halve the cucumbers widthways to make them shorter and easier to handle, then coarsely grate each one into the bowl the yogurt was straining over. Using clean hands (or a clean muslin cloth), squeeze as much of the liquid out of the cucumber as possible.
Add the strained, grated cucumber, garlic, and ¾ tsp flaky salt to the rest of the ingredients and mix well. Garnish with a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil and a sprinkling of dried mint.
On a large plate, mix the cajun spice and cayenne pepper with a little seasoning. Use this mixture to coat the fish all over.

Heat a little oil in a frying pan. Add in the fish and cook over a medium heat until golden. Reduce the heat and continue frying until the fish is cooked through, which should take about 10 minutes. Cook in batches if the pan is too crowded.
Meanwhile, prepare the dressing by combining all the ingredients with a little seasoning.

Soften the tortillas by heating them in the microwave for 5–10 seconds. Pile high the avocado, lettuce, and spring onion. Add a spoonful of salsa, top with large flakes of fish, and drizzle over the dressing.
Soak the salted fish in water overnight.
Next, heat the salted fish in water on the stove until the water boils. You should see a foam on top. Remove from heat, drain, and set aside. Shred the salted fish once it cools.
Cook bacon in a skillet over medium heat until crispy. Remove the bacon from heat and drain the majority of the bacon grease, leaving about 1 tablespoon in the skillet.
Add yellow onion, green onion, scotch bonnet pepper, and garlic to the skillet and stir. Cook for about 2 minutes or until the onions soften.
Add the salted fish to the skillet and stir. Cook for about a minute.
Next, add callaloo, Roma tomatoes, thyme, and black pepper. Stir to combine and cook until heated through, about 2 minutes.
Enjoy.
Cut the leaves and soft stems from the kale branches, then soak them in a bowl of cold water for about 5–10 minutes, or until finished with preparation.
Proceed to slice the onions, mince the garlic, and dice the tomatoes. Set aside.
Remove the kale from water and cut it into chunks.
Place the bacon in a saucepan and cook until crispy. Then add the onions, garlic, and thyme, and stir for about a minute or more.
Add the tomatoes, scotch bonnet pepper, and smoked paprika. Sauté for about 2–3 more minutes.
Finally, add the vegetables and salt, mix well, and steam for about 6–8 minutes or until the leaves are tender. Add a tiny bit of water as needed. Adjust seasonings and turn off the heat.

Using a sharp knife, cut both ends off the plantain. This makes it easy to grip the skin. Slit a shallow line down the long seam of the plantain; peel only as deep as the skin. Remove the plantain peel by pulling it back.
Slice the plantain into medium-sized, lengthwise slices and set aside.
Coat a large frying pan with cooking oil spray. Spray the tops of the plantains with a generous layer of oil spray and sprinkle with salt and freshly ground pepper.
Let the plantains fry on medium heat, shaking the frying pan to redistribute them every few minutes.
As the plantains brown, continue to add cooking oil spray, salt, and pepper (if needed) until they have reached the desired color and texture.
Remove and serve with kale.

meals.strMeal: Camaro Grelhado Com Molho Cru (Grilled Prawns with Green Onion Sauce)
strInstructions: Combine all the ingredients and grind them into a paste to make the sauce.
Thread the prawns onto the skewers and brush with the sauce.
Grill until done (they should lose their translucent color), cooking for about 3–4 minutes on each side.
Serve with extra sauce on the side.

meals.strMeal: Cambodian Stir-fried Morning Glory with Pork, Fermented Soybeans, and Garlic
strInstructions: In a wok or large pan, heat 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil over medium heat. Once the oil is hot, add the garlic and stir-fry for about 30 seconds, until fragrant but not burnt.
Add the minced pork to the pan and break it up with a spatula. Stir-fry until the pork is fully cooked and browned, about 2-3 minutes.
Stir in the fermented soybeans and cook for another minute, mixing it thoroughly with the pork.
Toss in the cut morning glory and stir-fry for 2-3 minutes to retain its bright green color and crisp texture.
Check the seasoning and adjust with fish sauce or sugar if needed. Transfer to a serving dish and serve immediately with steamed rice.
In a bowl, mix the flour, coconut milk, palm sugar, eggs, and salt.
Brush a waffle iron with oil.
Pour in the batter and cook over an open fire until light brown and crispy.
Preheat the oven to fan 170°C, conventional 190°C, or gas 5.

Roll out the pastry on a lightly floured surface so that it is slightly thinner than straight from the package. Cut out 18–20 rounds using a 7.5 cm fluted cutter, and re-roll the trimmings. Use the rounds to line two deep 12-hole tart tins (do not use muffin tins). If you only have a regular-sized, 12-hole tart tin, you will be able to make a few more slightly shallower tarts.
Beat the eggs in a large bowl and combine them with the rest of the ingredients, except the walnuts.

Tip this mixture into a pan and stir continuously for 3–4 minutes, until the butter melts, and the mixture bubbles and starts to thicken. It should be thick enough to coat the back of a wooden spoon. Do not overcook, and be sure to stir all the time, as the mixture can easily burn. Remove from the heat and stir in the nuts.
Spoon the filling into the unbaked tart shells so that the surface is level with the pastry. Bake for 15–18 minutes, or until set and pale golden.
Leave in the tin to cool for a few minutes before lifting out onto a wire rack. Serve warm or cold.
Brown the Beef: In a large pot, brown the beef cubes. Remove and set aside.
Sauté Vegetables: In the same pot, cook the onion until translucent. Add carrots, potatoes, and pumpkin, and cook for a few minutes.
Simmer: Return the beef to the pot. Add broth and dried apricots. Season with salt and pepper. Simmer for 1–2 hours, or until the beef is tender.
Serve: Enjoy hot, served with a crusty piece of bread.
Pro Tips:
Brown the beef in batches to ensure it gets a good sear, which adds depth to the stew's flavor.
Adding the fruits toward the end of cooking preserves their texture and adds a subtle sweetness to the dish.
Add the tamarind pulp and 1 cup of granulated sugar to a bowl and mash them together with a spoon or fork.
Take small amounts of the tamarind and sugar mixture and roll them by hand into small balls. Make them the size of a marble or slightly bigger, as you prefer.
Add the remaining granulated sugar to a flat surface, such as a plate or a sheet pan. Roll the tamarind balls in the granulated sugar until they are well-coated.
The tamarind balls can be eaten immediately, or you can let them set for a few hours or overnight. Allowing them to set will give them a firmer texture and a more crystallized sugar coating.
Wash and soak the toor dal in approximately 3 cups of water for at least one hour. (Note: The dal will double in volume after soaking.) Drain the water.
Cook the dal with 2-1/2 cups of water, adding salt and turmeric. Cook on medium-high heat until the texture is soft (approximately 30 minutes); the consistency should resemble a thick soup.
In a frying pan, heat the ghee. Add cumin seeds and mustard seeds. After the seeds crack, add bay leaves, green chili, ginger, and chili powder. Stir for a few seconds.
Add tomatoes, salt, and sugar. Stir and cook until the tomatoes are tender and mushy.
Add cilantro and garam masala. Cook for about one minute.
Pour the seasoned mixture over the dal. Mix well and cook for another minute.
Serve with Naan.
Preheat oven to 350°F (180°C). Grease an 8-inch (20 cm) square baking pan.

To make the date filling: Combine the dates and water in a medium saucepan over high heat and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low and simmer gently, stirring occasionally until the mixture thickens, which takes around 6 to 8 minutes. Remove from heat, stir in vanilla, and let cool. Set aside.

Combine the rolled oats, flour, sugar, baking soda, and salt in a food processor. Pulse 3 times (carefully, do not overmix; you want the mixture combined, but not powdery). Add the butter and pulse again until the mixture is crumbly in pea-sized clumps. Transfer the mixture to a mixing bowl and thoroughly mix in the butter.

Spread half of the oat mixture on the bottom of the prepared baking pan, pressing down to form a flat, even bottom layer. Pour the date mixture over the oat crust and spread it evenly. Layer with the remaining oat mixture and press down to flatten the top layer.

Bake in the center of the oven for 30 to 35 minutes, or until the top is golden brown. Let cool completely in the pan before cutting and serving. Date Squares keep well, covered in the refrigerator for up to a week, and frozen for 2 to 3 months.
Preparation: Add the onions to a sauté pan and fry for 5 minutes in olive oil until they start to brown.
Turn off the heat and add the meat, rice, pepper, paprika, dried vegetable stock, parsley, and basil. Mix all the ingredients together.

Carefully unwrap the vine leaves. Place one vine leaf on your hand with the base on the bottom of your palm and the point pointing toward the top. Add a second vine leaf with the point pointing downwards and the base of the leaf at the top of your fingers; there should be a good overlap. You can do this on a flat surface if preferred.

Place a spoonful of the meat and rice mixture in the middle of the leaves. Fold the bottom of the leaf over the mixture, fold in both sides, and roll the leaf upwards, sealing the mixture into a parcel shape.
Line the bottom of a deep saucepan with a layer of vine leaves, and then place the vine leaf parcel on top.
Make as many vine leaf parcels as the meat and rice mixture will allow, adding each one to the saucepan. Pack the parcels side by side until the saucepan is covered, then start another layer on top.

Cooking: Pour enough boiling water into the saucepan to just cover the top of the vine leaves and bring to a boil. Cover with a lid and let it simmer for 1.5 hours. All the water should be absorbed; however, top up with a little extra boiling water if this happens before the time is up.
Serving: Remove from the saucepan and allow it to cool for 10 minutes before serving.
Prepare the noodles according to the package instructions, then drain and set aside. In a small bowl or jug, combine all the ingredients for the sauce, and set aside.

Heat the oil in a large wok or frying pan over high heat. Stir-fry the garlic and chili for 20 seconds, until just starting to turn golden. Add the steak and stir-fry for 1 minute, until seared and starting to brown. Add the broccoli and stir-fry for another minute. Then, tip in the noodles and add the sauce. Stir-fry for another 1–2 minutes until well combined and piping hot.
Stir through the basil, and then turn off the heat. Serve immediately to avoid overcooking.

The day before you want to make the dish, scatter half the salt, half the garlic, and half of the herbs over the base of a small shallow dish. Lay the duck legs, skin-side up, on top, and then scatter over the remaining salt, garlic, and herbs. Cover the duck and refrigerate overnight. This can be done up to 2 days ahead.

Pour the wine into a saucepan that will snugly fit the duck legs in a single layer. Brush the salt off the duck legs and place them, skin-side down, in the wine. Cover the pan with a lid and place over medium heat. As soon as the wine starts to bubble, turn the heat down to the lowest setting and cook for 2 hours, checking occasionally that the liquid is just barely simmering. (If you own a heat diffuser, it would be good to use it here.) After 2 hours, the duck legs should be submerged in their own fat and the meat should feel incredibly tender when prodded. Leave to cool.

The duck legs are now cooked and can be eaten immediately, or you can follow the next step if you like them crisp. If you are preparing ahead, pack the duck legs tightly into a plastic container or jar and pour over the fat, but not the liquid at the bottom of the pan. Cover and leave in the fridge for up to a month, or freeze for up to 3 months. The liquid you are left with makes a tasty gravy, which can be chilled or frozen until needed.

To reheat and crisp up the duck legs, heat oven to 220°C/fan 200°C/gas 7. Remove the legs from the fat and place them, skin-side down, in an ovenproof frying pan. Roast for 30–40 minutes, turning halfway through, until brown and crisp. Serve with the reheated gravy, a crisp salad, and some crisp golden potatoes.
In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the milk, sugar, and baking soda. Cook over low heat, stirring constantly to prevent burning.
Continue to cook, stirring frequently, until the mixture becomes thick and caramel-colored (about 1–2 hours).
Stir in the vanilla extract.
Allow the dulce de leche to cool, then transfer it to a jar. It will thicken further as it cools.
Tip: Stir continuously and keep the heat low to prevent the milk from burning and sticking to the bottom of the pot.
Tip: Adding a drop of vanilla extract at the end of cooking enhances the flavor.
Transfer the almonds to a small bowl and pour over boiling water until just covered. Let them sit for 5 minutes, then drain them in a sieve and allow them to dry.
Preheat the oven to 180°C/160°C fan/Gas Mark 4. Line a deep, loose-based 20cm cake tin with baking parchment.
Place the butter in a large bowl and beat well until soft. Add the sugar and beat until light and fluffy. Stir in the orange zest and apricot jam.

Sieve together the flour and baking powder. Add the eggs to the creamed butter and sugar, a little at a time, beating well between each addition. If the mixture starts to curdle, stir in a little flour.
Add the remaining flour and ground almonds and mix well. Mix in the milk, and then add the dried fruit and cherries, mixing gently until combined.

Spoon the mixture into the prepared tin and spread it level using the back of a spoon. Arrange the whole almonds close together in neat circles on the top of the cake. Bake in the oven for 45 minutes.

Lower the oven temperature to 160°C/140°C fan/Gas Mark 3 and cook for a further 60–80 minutes. Check the cake after 50 minutes by inserting a wooden or metal skewer into the cake. When it is done, it should have just a few crumbs attached. Check every 10 minutes—it is important not to overcook this cake, as the center will be a little soft.

Once cooked, remove the cake briefly from the oven. Place the milk and sugar into a small pan and heat gently until the sugar has dissolved. Brush over the top of the cake and return it to the oven for 2–3 minutes. Remove and allow the cake to cool in the tin. When completely cool, remove it from the tin, wrap it in foil, and keep it for at least 2 days before cutting.

Add the flour, 150 gr of the sugar, a pinch of salt, and 1 egg yolk into the bowl of a food processor. Cut the butter into small cubes and add it to the bowl. Process until a firm dough forms. Do not over-process, as you do not want the dough to warm up. Check if it sticks by pinching it between your fingers.
Turn the dough out onto a piece of plastic wrap. Use your hands to form a round ball, rolling it into the plastic. Place it in the fridge for half an hour to cool.
Take a round baking tin of 22 cm diameter and cover it with baking paper. Brush the sides of the tin with butter.
Preheat the oven to 170˚C (340˚F).
Cut the apples into cubes and mix them with the raisins, the leftover sugar, and the cinnamon.

Place the cooled dough on a flat surface sprinkled with flour and roll it out into a thin sheet. Place this dough into the baking tin, covering the bottom and the sides thoroughly. (Alternatively, you can simply press the dough into the tin without rolling it out; this is often easier but results in a less smooth finish. Feel free to do whatever works best for you.)
Make sure to keep 1/4 of the dough separate to form the strips for the top.

Once the bottom and sides are covered with the dough, crumble the rusks over the bottom. You can also use breadcrumbs for this. Gently shake the crumbs to ensure they are distributed equally across the bottom.

Add the apple mixture and divide it evenly over the tin. Roll out the remaining dough and cut it into strips. Place these strips over the top of the pie in a diamond-shaped pattern. Brush the strips and sides with the other egg yolk, and place the pie in the preheated oven.
Bake the apple pie for about 1 hour, or until golden and cooked through. Let the pie cool in the tin, and ensure the sides are loose before opening the tin.

Place the flour in a large bowl and add the yeast and sugar. If the milk is cold, make sure to heat it to lukewarm. Mix the flour mixture bit by bit with the milk until you have a smooth batter. Add the salt and mix with a wooden spoon until the batter is smooth and liquidy.
Cover with a clean tea towel and leave in a warm spot for about an hour to rise.
Heat your deep fryer, or add the oil to a large pot. The oil should be at 180˚C (350˚F) for best results.

Before starting, stir your batter through, and then scoop the batter with two spoons or an ice cream scoop. Carefully let the batter drop into the oil. It is easiest to do this if you dip your spoons or scoop into the oil before dipping into the batter; that way, it will slide off the spoons easily. Do not overcrowd the fryer to prevent the oil from cooling down too quickly and everything from sticking together.
Once one side is golden brown, it usually turns over to the other side automatically. If not, help it along with two forks.
Take the balls out once golden brown and leave to drain on a piece of kitchen paper.
Mix the dry yeast with some of the lukewarm milk and stir until dissolved.
Place the buckwheat and the flour together in a bowl and make a small circle in the middle. Add the yeast mixture into it. Add the milk and stir until you have a smooth batter.
Add the eggs, salt, and vanille sugar, and stir through. Leave to stand and rise for about 45 minutes.

Heat the poffertjespan and add a bit of butter to each hole. Fill halfway with batter and first bake one side until you can see the top dry out a little. Turn the poffertjes around with a small fork and bake the other side until cooked and golden brown.
Serve the poffertjes with butter and icing sugar.
Preheat the oven to 160˚C (320˚F). Line a cake pan with parchment paper and set aside.
Mix all the dry ingredients together in a large bowl.
Place the milk in a small saucepan and heat it until it is almost boiling, but not quite.
Pour the milk slowly into the dry ingredients while whisking. Do not overmix.
As soon as it combines into a smooth batter, pour it into the cake pan.
Sprinkle the pearl sugar over the top and bake it in the oven for about 1 hour.
After about 50 minutes, check how the cake is progressing. If a cake tester comes out clean, the cake is cooked. If not, let it cook for a little longer.
Combine milk and yeast in a bowl. Let stand for a moment to allow the yeast to dissolve.
In another bowl, combine flour, butter, sugar, egg, and salt. Pour in the yeast mixture and knead until smooth. Cover the bowl and let the dough rise for one hour.

When the dough is almost ready, make the stroop filling. Combine all the ingredients in a saucepan and stir until the butter is melted and the sugar is dissolved. Let it simmer for a while. The stroop will continue to thicken as it cools.
Shape the dough into balls weighing about 35 grams each. Make a total of 14. Turn your stroopwafel iron on the highest setting.
Place a ball of dough in the iron and close the iron. Do not flatten the waffle too much; you should still be able to cut through it. Bake for 1–2 minutes until the waffle is nicely golden brown.
When the waffle is done, work quickly. Remove the waffle from the iron and immediately use a round cutter to cut out a nice circle of about 8 to 9 cm (3 to 3.5 inches).
Place the hot waffle on a cutting board and cut horizontally with a sharp knife. Since the stroopwafel is very hot, use an oven mitt to hold it in place.
Take half a waffle and spread the (hot!) stroop on it. Place the other half on top, pressing gently if necessary, and place the assembled waffle on a wire rack to cool. Repeat for all the balls.

Prepare the almonds the day before: Bring 6 cups of water to a boil. Remove from heat, and add the almonds. Let the almonds soak in water for about 5 minutes, then drain and peel. Spread the almonds on baking sheets, and bake at 200 degrees F (95 degrees C) until completely dry and toasted. This takes several hours, and needs to be prepared ahead. Be careful not to burn the nuts, as this will give a bitter taste to the filling.

Combine 1 cup sugar and 1 cup water in a saucepan, and bring to a boil. Add 1 teaspoon lemon juice, reduce heat to low, and let it simmer until syrupy, about 30 to 40 minutes. Stir in orange blossom water, and remove from heat. Set the sugar syrup aside.

Combine flour and salt in a large mixing bowl. Make a hole in the center, and pour in oil, egg, 1/2 teaspoon lemon juice, and 1 tablespoon orange blossom water into the center. Mix with fingers until the dough resembles coarse crumbs. Gradually sprinkle with warm water while mixing until the dough becomes soft and pliable. Divide into 4 equal portions. Cover the dough with a wet cloth, and set aside.

In a food processor, finely grind the almonds. Measure 3 cups of the finely ground almonds into a mixing bowl, and stir together with 1 cup sugar, baking powder, vanilla powder, lemon zest, and 2 tablespoons orange flower water. Mix in three eggs one at a time, stirring constantly, until you get a sticky, paste-like mixture.

Sprinkle cornstarch on the rolling surface to prevent sticking. Roll each portion of dough very thinly, 1 to 2 millimeters (1/16 inch). Cut the rolled dough into circles of about 10 centimeters (4 inches) in diameter each. Lightly wipe the surface of each circle with cornstarch, and fit into a tart mold, cornstarch side down to prevent sticking. Gently press the dough onto the sides and bottom of the mold, and trim extra dough from around the rim. Fill three quarters of each mold with the almond filling.

Bake on the top shelf at 350 degrees F (175 degrees C) for 20 to 25 minutes, or until the surface of the tart is golden and the dough is firm. Remove the tarts from the molds as soon as they come out of the oven. Dip each tart in the sugar syrup while still hot. Stick a pine nut into the middle of each tart for decoration, and place on a wire rack to drain.

Heat the oven to 200°C/180°C fan/Gas 6. Heat the olive oil in a large, ovenproof frying pan over medium heat. Fry the onion, chorizo, and peppers along with a pinch of salt and pepper for 15 minutes, until the vegetables have softened and the chorizo has released its oils. Add the paprika and garlic, and cook for another few minutes until fragrant.

Tip in the chopped tomatoes, olives, and butter beans. Stir to combine and season. Nestle in the chicken thighs and season well. Transfer to the oven and bake for 40 minutes, until the chicken skin is crisp and the meat is cooked through and tender. Scatter with the parsley and serve.
To make the pastry, dice the butter and place it in the freezer until very hard.
Add flour to the food processor bowl with half the butter and pulse until the mixture reaches the texture of breadcrumbs.
Pour in the lemon juice and 100ml iced water, and pulse until a dough forms.
Add the rest of the butter and pulse a few times until the dough is heavily flecked with butter. It is important not to overdo this, as the visible flecks of butter are what make the pastry flaky.
On a floured surface, roll the pastry out into a neat rectangle of approximately 20 x 30cm.
Fold the two ends of the pastry into the middle (see Picture 1), then fold it in half (Picture 2).
Roll the pastry out again and repeat this refolding process 3 more times. Rest the pastry for at least 15 minutes after each roll and fold.
Finally, refrigerate the pastry for at least 30 minutes before use.
To make the filling, melt the butter in a large saucepan.
Remove the saucepan from the heat and stir in all the remaining ingredients until completely mixed. Set aside.
To make the cakes, roll the pastry out until it is slightly thicker than a £1 coin and cut out 8 rounds, approximately 12cm across. Re-roll any trimming if necessary.
Place one good heaped tablespoon of the filling mixture in the center of each round.
Brush the edges of the rounds with water, then gather the pastry around the filling and gently squeeze it closed (Picture 3).
Flip the cakes over so the smooth top is upwards, and pat them into a smooth round shape.
Flatten each round with a rolling pin until it is oval, stopping when the fruit just begins to poke through. Place the cakes on a baking tray.
Cut 2 small slits into each Eccles cake, brush generously with egg white, and sprinkle with sugar (Picture 4).
Preheat the oven to 220C/200C fan/gas 8.
Bake the Eccles cakes for 15–20 minutes, or until they are just past golden brown and sticky.
Let them cool on a rack and enjoy them while still warm or cold with a cup of tea.
If desired, Eccles cakes also pair well with a wedge of hard, tangy British cheese such as Lancashire or cheddar.
Add the chicken broth to a wok and bring it to a boil.
Next, add the salt, sugar, white pepper, and sesame seed oil.
Once the chicken broth is boiling, add the vegetables to the wok.
To thicken the sauce, whisk together 1 Tablespoon of cornstarch and 2 Tablespoons of water in a separate bowl. Slowly pour this mixture into the soup until it reaches the desired thickness.
Next, add 1 egg, slightly beaten with a knife or fork. Slowly pour the egg into the soup and stir for 8 seconds.
Serve the soup in a bowl and garnish with green onions.
Make the gravy: In a small saucepan, add the beef stock, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, oyster sauce, and white pepper powder. Heat over medium heat, whisk together, and bring to a simmer.

Combine the cornstarch and water in a small bowl and whisk to dissolve. Pour this mixture into the saucepan and whisk until the gravy thickens and coats the back of the spoon (2–3 minutes). Cover the saucepan and keep it warm on the lowest possible heat.

Make the egg foo young batter: In a medium bowl, whisk together the cornstarch and water until dissolved. Add the eggs, salt, and sugar. Whisk until well combined and there are no remaining egg white clumps.
Add the green onion, bean sprouts, and shrimp. Stir until all ingredients are evenly coated.

Fry the egg foo young: Add vegetable oil to a large wok; the oil should reach about 2 inches up the sides. Heat the oil over medium-high heat to 350°F, or until vigorous bubbles form around an inserted wooden chopstick.

Using a ladle, gently and slowly add 1/4 of the omelet batter. The egg foo young should immediately bubble and puff up. Fry until golden brown and crispy on each side, about 2 minutes per side. If any light spots appear, use a ladle to gently baste the omelet with hot oil.

Tip: Egg foo young can be tricky to flip. The easiest method is to use tools in each hand (a spider, slotted spoon, tongs, or large chopsticks are all good candidates) and gently coax the omelet over, pulling up on one side while pushing down and around on the other.

Remove the omelet and place it on a paper towel-lined baking sheet. Let it cool for 5 minutes. Meanwhile, repeat the process with the remaining batter to make four omelets, adding more oil to the pan between batches if needed.
Serve and enjoy: Plate each egg foo young over a bed of rice. Spoon the warm gravy over the top and serve immediately.

Slice 1 lb. of small Japanese or Italian eggplant (about 3) into quarters lengthwise, then cut crosswise into 2-inch-wide pieces. Place in a medium bowl. Add 1 Tbsp. of sugar, 1 tsp. of Diamond Crystal or ½ tsp. of Morton kosher salt, and ½ tsp. of freshly ground black pepper. Toss to evenly coat the eggplant and let it sit at room temperature for at least 20 minutes, up to 2 hours.

Peel and thinly slice 8 garlic cloves. Add 3 Tbsp. of vegetable oil and half of the garlic to a medium Dutch oven or other heavy pot. Cook over medium-high heat, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon, until light golden and crisp, about 5 minutes. Using a slotted spoon, transfer the garlic chips to a plate and season lightly with salt.

Place 4 oz. of ground pork in the same pot and break it up into small pieces with a wooden spoon. Season with ¼ tsp. of Diamond Crystal or Morton kosher salt and cook, undisturbed, until deeply browned underneath, about 5 minutes. Using a slotted spoon, transfer the pork to another plate, leaving fat behind in the pot.
Place the eggplant on a clean kitchen towel and blot away any moisture the salt has drawn out.
Working in batches and adding more oil if needed, cook the eggplant in the same pot until lightly browned, about 3 minutes per side. Transfer the cooked eggplant to a plate with the pork.

Pour 1½ cups of water into the pot and scrape up browned bits from the bottom with a wooden spoon. Add the remaining garlic, 3 Tbsp. of coconut vinegar or unseasoned rice vinegar, 2 Tbsp. of soy sauce, 2 bay leaves, 1 tsp. of freshly ground black pepper, and the remaining 1 Tbsp. of sugar. Bring to a simmer, then return the pork and eggplant to the pot. Reduce heat to medium-low, partially cover, and simmer until the eggplant is tender and silky and the sauce is reduced by half, 20–25 minutes. Taste and season with more salt and pepper, and add a little more sugar if needed.
Top with the garlic chips and serve with cooked white rice.

To prepare the bread base: Take pita bread and rip it into bite-sized pieces. In a frying pan, add about a 1/4 stick of butter, add the bread pieces, and fry until golden brown and crisp. Transfer these pieces to a glass baking dish, preferably a square-sized dish, and set aside.

To continue the bread base: Add a little more butter to the same pan. Stir in salt, approximately 2 cloves of crushed fresh garlic, and a teaspoon of cumin. Stir around until you can smell the aroma. Then, add the fried bread pieces to this mixture and stir to coat the bread. Return the pieces to the glass baking dish and set aside.

To prepare the meat: Place some butter in a pot. Stir-fry the meat until brown. Add 1 quartered onion, salt, pepper, 1 cube of chicken bouillon, and water to cover the meat. Bring to a boil, then turn down to a simmer. Cover and cook until tender, approximately 2 hours. After the meat has cooled, remove the chunks of meat and place them in a bowl; set aside. Reserve the soup liquid from the meat separately.

To prepare the rice: Put some butter into a pot and add shareya (fideo noodles), roughly a handful. Keep stirring until golden brown—not too dark, but very golden. Then add two cups of rice and stir a little until some of the rice turns an opaque white. Add 2-1/4 cups of water and salt to taste. Bring to a boil, cover, and turn down to a simmer. Cook until tender. Test the rice tenderness after about 35 minutes.

Assemble the dish: Add some of the reserved meat soup to the bread pieces in the baking dish until saturated. Add the cooked rice on top of the bread pieces. Slowly spoon the remainder of the soup onto the rice, aiming for a level that reaches just to the top of the rice. Do not worry if the level is not exact.

To prepare the red sauce: In a pan, add a little oil or butter. Add crushed tomato, a half teaspoon of tomato paste, salt, pepper, 2 cloves of fresh crushed garlic, and cumin. Add also approximately 3 tablespoons of vinegar. Stir this mixture until you smell the aroma and it is a bit smooth. It should be a bit thick, not watery, but if it is too thick, you can add a bit of water. Spread the sauce with a wooden spoon atop the rice to cover.

To fry the meat topping: In a pan, add a bit of butter or oil, the meat, a touch of tomato paste, about a tablespoon of fresh crushed garlic, salt, pepper, and a teaspoon of cumin. Cook until the meat is golden fried.
Spoon the fried meat topping atop the rice. Serve and enjoy!

**Make the Dough:** Mix flour and salt in a large bowl. Add the butter and use your fingers to blend it into a crumbly texture. Gradually add water, mixing until a dough forms. Wrap the dough and chill for 30 minutes.

**Prepare the Filling:** Cook the onions in a pan until translucent. Add the ground beef and cook until browned. Stir in the spices, then remove the mixture from the heat. Once cooled, mix in the eggs and olives.
**Assemble:** Roll out the dough and cut it into circles. Place one spoonful of filling in the center of each circle, fold over, and seal the edges completely.
**Cook:** Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 20–25 minutes, or until golden brown.

**Pro Tips:** For a flakier crust, incorporate one tablespoon of vinegar into the dough mixture; this helps prevent gluten formation. Also, seal the edges of the empanadas with a fork to ensure they do not open during baking or frying.
Heat the flat grill plate over low heat, preferably on top of two rings/flames if space permits, and brush sparingly with light olive oil.

Cook the sausages first. Add the sausages to the hot grill plate (or the coolest part, if available) and allow them to cook slowly for about 15–20 minutes, turning occasionally, until golden. After the first 10 minutes, increase the heat to medium before beginning to cook the other ingredients. If you are struggling for space, completely cook the sausages and keep them hot in the oven on a plate.

Snip a few small cuts into the fatty edge of the bacon. Place the bacon directly on the grill plate and fry for 2–4 minutes each side, or until your preferred crispiness is reached. Like the sausages, the cooked bacon can be kept hot in the oven on a plate.

For the mushrooms, brush away any dirt using a pastry brush and trim the stalks level with the mushroom tops. Season with salt and pepper and drizzle with a little olive oil. Place them stalk-side up on the grill plate and cook for 1–2 minutes before turning and cooking for a further 3–4 minutes. Avoid moving the mushrooms too much while cooking, as this releases the natural juices, making them soggy.

For the tomatoes, cut them across the center (or in half lengthways if using plum tomatoes). With a small, sharp knife, remove the green "eye." Season with salt and pepper and drizzle with a little olive oil. Place cut-side down on the grill plate and cook without moving for 2 minutes. Gently turn over and season again. Cook for a further 2–3 minutes until tender but still holding their shape.
For the black pudding, cut it into 3–4 slices and remove the skin. Place on the grill plate and cook for 1½–2 minutes each side until slightly crispy.

For 'proper' fried bread, it is best to cook it in a separate pan. Ideally, use bread that is a couple of days old. Heat a frying pan to medium heat and coat the base with oil. Add the bread and cook for 2–3 minutes each side until crispy and golden. If the pan becomes too dry, add a little more oil. For a richer flavor, add a knob of butter after you turn the slice.

For the fried eggs, break the egg directly into the pan with the fried bread and let it sit for 30 seconds. Add a good knob of butter and lightly splash/baste the egg with the melted butter. Cook to your preferred stage, season, and gently remove with a fish slice.
Once all the ingredients are cooked, serve on warm plates and enjoy straight away with a good squeeze of tomato ketchup or brown sauce.

Pour 230ml lukewarm water into a bowl and add the yeast. Let it stand for 3 minutes. Then, add the caster sugar, eggs, flour, and 1 tsp sea salt flakes. Mix together to form a dough, then knead for 10 minutes in a stand mixer using a dough hook (or 15 minutes by hand) until the dough is elastic enough to be almost see-through when stretched. Cover and set aside to rest for 30 minutes. Cut the dough into four equal pieces and transfer them to a baking tray lined with baking parchment. Let them rest for another 30 minutes.

Oil the work surface and a rolling pin with vegetable oil. Working with one portion of dough at a time, flatten it against the surface using the palm of your hand, then roll it out into a thin rectangle, about 30 x 50cm. Let it rest for 2 minutes while you spread a quarter of the lard over the top. (If you want to fill your pastry with sobrasada de Mallorca, mix 50g lard with the sobrasada, and spread this over the dough instead.) Pull one corner of the flattened dough and stretch it out as far as it will go without breaking. Repeat every 10cm or so around the dough rectangle in every direction until it reaches about 50 x 70cm.

Cut a strip from each of the shorter sides and lay these beside each other along one of the longer sides of the rectangle; this is what we call the heart of the ensaimada. From there, begin rolling the dough until you have a long pastry snake. Repeat with the remaining portions of dough.

Take the first roll of dough and stretch it until it is over a metre long. Then, roll it up into a spiral, leaving 1cm between each turn of the spiral so the dough can expand. Flatten it a little and transfer it to a baking sheet lined with baking parchment. Repeat with the remaining dough. Let it rise in a warm place for at least 12 hours, or ideally 24 hours.

Heat the oven to 200C/180C fan/gas 6. Place the ensaimadas in the top third of the oven and immediately reduce the temperature to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Bake for 18 minutes until dark golden. Let them cool on a wire rack. To fill your ensaimada with whipped cream, slice and open it, then spread the cream over it and close. Dust with a generous amount of icing sugar, if you like, and cut into pieces to serve.

Rinse the fish. Rub it with lemon or lime, seasoned with salt and pepper, or use your favorite seasoning (I used creole seasoning). Set aside, or place it in the oven to keep warm until the sauce is ready.

In a large skillet, heat oil over medium heat until hot. Add the fish and cook each side for about 5–7 minutes until cooked through and crispy on both sides. Remove the fish and set aside. Drain the oil, leaving about 2–3 tablespoons of oil.
Add the bay leaf, garlic, and ginger. Stir-fry for about one minute, making sure the garlic does not burn.
Next, add the onion, bell peppers, thyme, scotch bonnet, sugar, and allspice. Continue stirring for about 2–3 minutes. Add the vinegar and mix. Adjust salt and pepper according to preference.
Let the mixture simmer for about 2 more minutes.
Discard the bay leaf and thyme sprigs. Serve the sauce over the fish with a side of bammy. You may make the sauce up to 2 days in advance.
Purée half the strawberries in a blender. Chop the remaining strawberries, reserving four for decoration.
Whip the double cream until stiff peaks form, then fold in the strawberry purée and crushed meringue.
Fold in the chopped strawberries and ginger cordial, if using.
Spoon equal amounts of the mixture into four cold wine glasses.
Serve, garnished with the remaining strawberries and a sprig of mint.

Put the tomatoes and all of the peppers in a food processor and blitz until finely chopped. Tip the mixture into a sieve, set over a bowl, and leave to strain. Add the onions, garlic, and parsley to the food processor and blitz until finely chopped, then set aside.

Add red pepper paste, tomato purée, pomegranate molasses, pul biber, sumac, dried mint, and most of the extra virgin olive oil to a serving bowl and whisk well until everything comes together as a sauce. Tip in the blitzed onion mixture and the strained pepper mixture, along with 1 tsp flaky sea salt. Stir well, then drizzle with the remaining extra virgin olive oil to serve.
